extra.reasoning:
The property is the invariant: opted_out=true implies sent=false. I checked whether any reachable state can have both opted_out=true and sent=true.

sent is set to true by exactly ONE transition: t2 (send_attempt), whose guard is "not opted_out". So whenever sent becomes true, opted_out is false in that resulting state.

opted_out is set to true by exactly ONE transition: t1 (optout), which simultaneously sets sent:=false. So whenever opted_out becomes true, sent is set false in the same step.

Every other transition either (a) sets sent:=false (t3-t26 key events, and t27 send_attempt-when-opted_out) and never touches opted_out, or (b) is t2 which only fires when not opted_out.

Inductive proof of invariant (opted_out -> not sent):
- Initial: opted_out=false, invariant trivially holds.
- t1: post-state opted_out=true, sent=false -> holds.
- t2: enabled only when not opted_out -> post-state opted_out=false -> antecedent false -> holds.
- t3-t26: opted_out unchanged, sent:=false -> if opted_out true then sent false -> holds.
- t27: opted_out unchanged, sent:=false -> holds.

In every successor state, either opted_out is false or sent is false. There is no way to make opted_out true while leaving sent true, because the only opted_out:=true transition also forces sent:=false, and the only sent:=true transition requires not opted_out. Therefore no reachable state (within 11 events or unbounded) violates the property. The property holds.
